name: Ykrul
description:
Ykrul

Key Demographics: Ykrul

Capital City: Midh-Kakorum

Entanglements

To the South East:
Aeler – The mountain passes are sealed by dwarvish tariffs and breath-counters; the Khagan's word is the only passport. Iron flows south; grain flows north when the snows permit.
To the South West:
Vilikari / Acasia – The Foedus Stone and the grass-lands are the eternal negotiation; raids settle what treaties cannot. The hostage strings are the true currency of the border.
To the South:
Ecktoria – Legates tally supplies as Ykrul riders stand, silhoettes across the sea of grass.
To the North:
The Iron Grass (Kuvani) – Remount clans and rival khagans watch the horizon; alliances shift with the seasons. The wind carries the names of the dead; the dead carry the grudges.

Quick Reference

Tagline: The steppe owes nothing. The wolf pays no debts. Only the fool plays for beauty; the wise play for the exit.

Khatun Bortai of the Ring (Elite):

“We do not dream on the steppe. We count. We measure breath, grain, and the length of a blade. My grandfather taught me Kon'reh before I could hunt: the board is dirt, the pieces are sheep-bones, and the lesson is always the same—the game ends when one side has no roads left. Politics is merely the same game played with living pieces. To lead here is to know when to trade, when to raid, and when to ride away. Sentiment is a luxury of farmers. We are the children of the horse, the wind, and the knife.”

Herd-Scout Sogata (Commoner):

“Kon'reh teaches that the straight road is a lie. The wise shepherd knows the river bends not from weakness, but from wisdom. We are practical folk. A horse that cannot carry you is meat. An oath that cannot be kept is smoke. Speak true, ride steady, and always count your exits before you enter. The steppe does not forgive the beautiful mistake—only the useful truth.”

Genre / Mood: Nomadic epic, pragmatic cunning, wind-scoured honor, Scythian steppe.

Starting Location: A winter camp on the steppe edge, banners snapping in frost-bright wind as the Khagan's fires burn low in the east. A Khatun of the Ring offers a clay cup of salted tea. “The Khagan's kin ride from the salt pan. The old sayings say the straight road is a lie. You came by the bent path—the wolf knows you. Speak your errand before the wind changes. Trade is welcome; lies are tallied.”

Theme & Atmosphere

The Ykrul ride the endless Violet Steppe, where the horizon drinks the sky and survival is a game played without mercy. They are a pragmatic people—harder and more practical than the settled folk of the south. Sentiment is a waste of breath; beauty is a fool's consolation. Their philosophy is not written in books but scratched in the dirt boards of Kon'reh, a game of encirclement, escape, and blockade played with pebbles and sheep-bones. Every child learns before they can hunt: count your exits before you enter, never commit to a single path, and an ally untested is a blade without a sheath. This is not mysticism. It is arithmetic. The steppe is a hard teacher, and the Ykrul are its starkest students.

What outsiders call “geometry” the Ykrul call the Count—the simple, bone-deep understanding that every route has a price, every camp has a weakness, and every alliance is only as strong as the remounts that sustain it. Their law lives in saddlebags. Every crossing has its toll, every ford its depth, every feud a reckoning measured in horse-lengths and heartbeats. Hosts gather by the thousand, then scatter at a word; one night you share their fires, the next they test your bonds. Their banners are wolves' teeth, their treaties braided cords, their memory longer than the rivers that cut the grass. The old folk still recite the Thirty-Six Sayings of the Road, but the wisest know the thirty-seventh is the one you learn alone, on a night when the stars blink out and the wolf does not howl.

What you notice first: The smell of horse sweat and woodsmoke. The way every rider counts hoofbeats under their breath. The silence when a stranger approaches the fire. The cold assessment in every eye—not hostile, merely reckoning value.

The one rule that kills newcomers: Never ride between two wolves at dusk. The space between them is a snare with no exit. In Kon'reh it is called the Closed Circle; in life it is called death.

The Ninth Taboo Manifestation

In any Ykrul camp, the ninth cup poured at a feast is for the ancestors. The wise pour eight and leave the ninth as a pause. Those who pour the ninth cup find that the ancestors drink—and the ancestors are always thirsty. The next morning, the pourer's shadow is one inch shorter. If the same person pours the ninth cup nine times, their shadow vanishes entirely, and they become a ghost rider, forever counting cups at the edge of the firelight.

Regional Mood: The Pragmatism of the Steppe

Here, the horizon is not a line—it is a reckoning. The Ykrul measure distance in oaths and remounts, not miles. A camp is a weave of debts owed and honor satisfied, audited by the Camp-Mother's sharp eyes. A feud is a slow equation: blood in, blood out, with interest compounded by winter. The Khagan rules not by force alone but by keeping the old calculations—the unwritten rules that govern every crossing, every treaty, every duel. Break the pattern, and the steppe itself may turn against you. Keep it, and even enemies will share their salt, though they will count the grains. The Ykrul build no monuments. They build relationships, each one a stone in an invisible cairn that stretches from the Dolmis to the Aberderrin, and each one valued only so long as it holds weight.

Prominent NPCs of the Ykrul

|p0.25|p0.3|p0.15|}

Name

Role/TitleMotivationRumoured Quirk
Khatun BortaiKeeper of the RingKeep the clans fed and mounted; let poets starveHer hostage strings are braided from the hair of oath-breakers.
Noyan TemurBanner-lord of the Red ReedsWin a winter pasture without losing a single man to talkHe has never lost a negotiation. He has never needed to speak.
SogataHerd-Scout (commoner exemplar)Read the weather before the storm because a wet horse is a dead horseHe can read a trail by the way the grass bends—and a liar by the way a rider checks his exits.
Elder SaruulBone-SingerKeep the names of the dead so debts are not forgottenShe sings the names of the departed each dawn; the dead sing back.
The Dust PrinceRaider who rides the wind's leeAmass enough salt to buy a clan because gold is too heavy to run withHe offers escort braids for secrets, never seen from the front.
Storm-Singer UrumSky-SpeakerSell weather on credit because even the sky is a resourceHis bills come due during the Wolf Moon.

Names of the Ykrul

Inspired by the open steppe and the deep east: guttural stops, flowing vowels, and suffixes that mark lineage or deed.

KhasarAltanNoyanthe Unbuckled
MongkeChechegBorjiginStorm-Born

(Steppe Road/Winter Ring/Ford)

Wolf Road milepost of stacked stones with wind whining through eye-holes; winter camp ring with felt tents in horseshoe facing wind.

Using the Ykrul Deck

Draw until you have all four suits: Spade = place, Heart = actor, Club = pressure, Diamond = leverage. Highest rank sets the main Timer (2–5 $$ 4, 6–10 $$ 6, J/Q/K $$ 8, A $$ 10).

Diamonds are codified outcomes (passes/remounts/truces) that change position rather than call for a roll.

If any Ace appears, echo steppe and sky motifs—wind that speaks, horses that remember, treaties that bind across generations, and the cold arithmetic of Kon'reh made manifest.

Spades — Places

Steppe Road, Winter Ring, Ford

Hearts — People & Factions

Host, Envoy, Rider

Clubs — Complications/Threats

Law, Weather, Feud

Diamonds — Rewards/Leverage

Pass, Remount, Truce

Ykrul Mechanics

Core Rules

Kon'reh — The Game of the Steppe

The Ykrul do not speak of “geometry.” They speak of Kon'reh, a dirt-board game of encirclement, escape, and blockade played with pebbles and bone. Every Ykrul child learns its lessons: count your exits before you enter; never commit to a single path; a screen of light pieces can trap a heavy king; the best move is the one your opponent thinks you have forgotten. In Ykrul culture, Kon'reh is not recreation—it is education. A war-council may be fought out on a Kon'reh board before a single arrow is nocked. A treaty may be argued as a series of moves and counter-moves. When a Ykrul says “the Count,” they mean the cold reckoning of advantage: remounts, routes, grain-days, and the price of a blade in winter.

In play: When a Ykrul NPC evaluates an offer or a threat, they are playing Kon'reh in their head. Social rolls against Ykrul suffer +1 DV unless the player can demonstrate a “route of escape” (an exit, a concession, or a hostage) that makes the deal survivable for both sides.

Hostage Strings [4]

Every oath is secured with a hostage string—a token kin, retainer, or sworn ally left in the other camp. If the deal is broken, the hostage's fate becomes the story's next beat. Mechanically: create a 4-segment Hostage timer for any major treaty. On breach, the timer becomes a countdown to vengeance. Ykrul take hostages as a matter of course; refusing to give one is treated as a declaration of bad faith.

Steppe Law

Travelers are bound by steppe law: hospitality lasts three nights, insults last three generations. The Keeper may demand 1 SB when either law is broken. The three nights are measured by the Camp-Mother's fire; the three generations are measured by the Bone-Singer's memory.

Sky Omens

When the sky shows strange signs—mock-suns, green fire, thunder without storm—the steppe itself speaks. Once per scene, players may treat an omen as a Diamond, shifting Position without a roll. The GM may bank 1 SB for future weather complications. Pragmatic Ykrul treat omens as weather-data misread by the fearful; even so, they do not ride against them.

Bowl and Board

Ykrul elders settle disputes not by shouting but by drawing two circles in the dirt: the Bowl for fairness, then the Board for routes and concessions. A successful Wits + Lore (or relevant skill) roll creates a Seasonal Concession String at a ford, harbor, or pass. On a miss, the Market Moot [4] timer advances as factions argue.

Local Patrons

  • The Hoof — The Traveler. The path that never ends, the remount that never tires.
  • The Wolf's Tooth — The Witness. Every oath is counted. Every broken promise is a scar.
  • The Salt Throat — Maelstraeus. The weight that salts your tongue; the interest that blinds your horses.
  • The Sky Father — Kuva. The wind that carries names; the storm that erases them.

The Thirty-Seven Sayings of the Road (Excerpts)

The Ykrul teach strategy not in manuals but in proverbs—each a line of play from Kon'reh. A few of the most quoted:

Let the river freeze before you cross. — Do not rush. Let the opponent make the first move; your opening will be built on their impatience.

Borrow a corridor to trap a king. — When the enemy carves a path toward you, let it deepen. Then seal the mouth behind them.

Loot a burning house. — Strike only when the enemy is committed elsewhere.

Hide your dagger behind a smile. — Threaten one flank; strike the other.

The reed bends, the oak breaks. — Yield to overwhelming force, but do not break; preserve your core.

A distant banner casts no shadow. — A threat far away is no threat at all; control the center.

The guest who outstays his welcome steals the host's peace. — Allow no force, however small, to remain within your lines uncontrolled.

Count exits, not victims. — Before you move, count the ways your foe can flee. If the number is greater than zero, you have not yet won.

Superstitions (burn for +1 die once)

  • Salt to the Wind: Toss a pinch into the air before a long ride. Ignore the first White Squall penalty.
  • Knot the String: Tie a single knot in a hostage cord. Cancel Feud Spark or take –1 die on your next oath (your choice).
  • Name to the Kurgan: Whisper a dead enemy's name into a horse's ear. Gain +1 Effect when raiding their clan this scene, but mark Obligation to the Bone-Singer.

Strain Budget (SB) Spends

Minor (1 SB)

  • Exposure: Road-judges or border riders take notice.
  • Noise: A nearby camp or raiding party is alerted.
  • Trace: Hoofprints or broken grass mark your route for feud-kin.
  • Delay: A horse throws a shoe, a rope breaks—lose time.
  • Supply Strain: Mark +1 segment on fodder or water timer.

Moderate (2 SB)

  • Alarm Raised: A watch kopje signals; riders are coming.
  • Position Lost: Rasputitsa or white squall hides the trail.
  • Foe Appears: A raid shadow or hostage taker arrives.
  • Gear Trouble: A saddle snaps, a bowstring frays.
  • Lock/Barrier: A river breaks, a ford becomes impassable.

Serious (3 SB)

  • Reinforcements: A wolf muster or kurultai war party arrives.
  • Key Gear Breaks: A horse goes lame, a paiza cracks.
  • Major Twist: A grassfire cuts off retreat, or a feud spark escalates.
  • Rail Tick: Advance a campaign timer (e.g. Hostage Crisis).
  • Condition Applied: Mark Exhausted or Marked by Feud.

Major (4+ SB)

  • Trap Springs: A hidden kurgan collapses, a pontoon sinks.
  • Authority Arrival: Khatun of the Ring, Khagan's Kin, or Sky-Speaker intervenes.
  • Scene Shift: A sky omen rewrites the terrain; paths appear or vanish.
  • Patron Omen: A wolf watches, a horse speaks, the wind carries a name.
  • Narrative Pivot: The feud is not what it seemed; the hostage was a willing sacrifice.

Region-Specific SB Options

  • Steppe Law: Hospitality fails, insults echo, treaties shift mid-parley.
  • Horse Culture: Mounts refuse to move, remount posts are empty, riding rolls suffer –1 die.
  • Sky Omens: Weather changes instantly, stars point the wrong way, a raven shadows the party.

Extensions (Plug-in)

Pursuit Ladder (Near / Mid / Far)

  • Advance/Withdraw (Wits + Ride): On a hit, shift one band. On a strong hit, also impose Dust Cloud.
  • Cut the Herd (Presence + Sway): On a hit, freeze range for one exchange; if a remount chit is in your possession, +1 die.
  • Weather the Squall (Endurance + Survival): On a hit in White Squall, you pick who keeps their bearings; on a miss, the GM does.

Terrain Stock (d20)

Roll or choose a striking detail to dress a hex of the Violet Steppe.

  1. Wolf milepost with fresh red thread; someone retied the law.
  2. Reed ford poles lying on bank; current whispers a different depth.
  3. Fallen standard tooth used as a chopping block; grooves still warm.
  4. Circle of pony skulls facing east; one blinks when the wind shifts.
  5. Snow trench with cudgel steps; no prints leave it.
  6. Copper salt-scale hung from a birch; pans balance only under oath.
  7. Hoof-bells threaded on hair; ringing calls riders—or storms.
  8. Abandoned paiza shard wedged in a milestone; edges bite skin.
  9. Kurgan capstone chalked with a name misspelled; feud waiting.
  10. Flint knapper's tarp half-buried; a perfect arrowhead points north.
  11. Blue-fletched signal arrow stuck in frost; message in the vane.
  12. Camp-sweep marks: three rings—last night was a council.
  13. Wheel rut frozen like glass; a face looks up from inside.
  14. Salt-brined jerky hanging from a tripod; untouched by birds.
  15. River ice singing in thirds; ferryman's pole wedged under.
  16. Pony hobble-rope knotted in hostage-string pattern.
  17. Cold forge dug into loess; tools wrapped in wolfskin.
  18. Child's braid-bead dropped in snow; still warm to the tongue.
  19. Thorn-tether scratched with clan brands; two over-scratched.
  20. Bone flute lodged in a cairn; playing it summons a road-judge.

Lair Seeds (One-Page Rides)

  1. Ger of Echoes — Empty felt walls repeat anything uttered in the Khagan's voice once per night.
  2. Salt-Pit Shrine — Rope winch to brine caverns; offerings are tears and horsehair.
  3. Remount Vault — Subterranean stables under reed mats; mounts won't leave without a tale.
  4. Kite-Maker's Bluff — Bone kites pinned on racks; their shadows scout for raiders.
  5. Storm-Rider's Stone — Pillar that hums to hooves; racing round it sets weather wagers.
  6. Frozen Court — Ice dais in a dry riverbed; law held here counts triple until thaw.
  7. Banner Grave — Flags planted like trees; each knows the battle it died in.
  8. String-Mother's Yard — Web of hostage cords; cutting one opens a secret but starts a feud.
  9. Fire-Under-Snow — Warm vent where foals winter; spirits ask for salt-song.
  10. Quiet Palisade — Trading post where voices fail; deals done in gesture and cut meat.
  11. Ashen Trident — Three lightning-split birches; tie a braid to choose a road's luck.
  12. Gray Ger — Moves at night; inside, a camp-mother tests your hospitality the other way around.

Steppe Bestiary (Palette)

  • Steppe Manticores — Lionine torsos, porcupine tails of bone quills. Signs: quills in fence-posts; ponies refuse to drink. Hook: play a raid-song backward; they circle to listen.
  • Dust Hulks — Wind-packed silhouettes that rise from dunes. Signs: dunes “breathe”; grit sifts upward. Hook: spill water on their shadow; they settle for a watch.
  • Bone Kites — Carrion birds with rune-etched pinions. Signs: spiral flights over kurgans. Hook: offer a name-stake (twig with hair) to barter silence.
  • Frost Wargs — Blue-pelt wolves that steam cold. Signs: rime on tracks; breath that crackles. Hook: warm milk poured on ice buys one unchallenged crossing.
  • Reed Stalkers — Long-necked marsh hunters, gait like stilts. Signs: pole-pocks where no ferryman walks. Hook: carry reeds in your mouth; they read it as truce.
  • Salt Screamers — Pale eels in dry pans; voices like flutes. Signs: fissures crusted with salt frost. Hook: scatter ash; they coil to sing and forget to bite.

Relic & Find (d10)

When you uncover a kurgan cache or a lost hoard, roll or pick:

  1. Wolf-tooth standard tip; planted, it demands silence for parley.
  2. Bronze paiza half; merchants honor it as full once—then talk.
  3. Salt-needle that points to the nearest brine store.
  4. Frost-bitten stirrup; mount that wears it ignores ice once.
  5. Braid-bead of hostage glass; melts when a string is cut nearby.
  6. River-depth rod carved with safe counts for three fords.
  7. Iron hoof-bell that rings for liars at council.
  8. Bone-lot cup; roll it to name which feud clause applies.
  9. Wind-wrung scarf; pass it round to share breath in a white squall.
  10. Wolf-road tablet scrap; reads as a Foedus Seal to one clerk.

Complication Twists (d8)

When a travel complication arises, roll or choose a twist that deepens the story.

  1. White Squall reveals a remount vault's smoke hole; entering angers reed stalkers.
  2. Rasputitsa exposes kurgan stones; riding around them starts a name-claim.
  3. Feud Spark was staged by a salt-broker; paying in Salt Allotment unmasks them.
  4. Hostage Call is for a Safe-Hostage you already carry—by another name.
  5. Grassfire runs uphill toward a council hollow; smoke votes before riders do.
  6. Foedus Recall cites a river you crossed yesterday—now it flows the other way.
  7. Raid Shadow is your own tracks, mirrored by sky omens; choose which you admit.
  8. Kurultai Call stacks councils; words spoken in one bind you in the other.

Boss Seeds (Three-Phase Foes)

  • The String-Mother (Nine Hostages) — Phases: Knot $$ Tighten $$ Sever. Cracks: return a hostage string, win a Bowl and Board arbitration, cut the right knot.
  • The Dust Prince (Rider of the Red Wind) — Phases: Raid Shadow $$ Salt Tithe $$ Kurultai Coup. Cracks: spill a salt allotment in his path, unmask his face before the council, outride him through a white squall.

Lore Echoes (Cross-Expansion Hooks)

  • The Bitter Root: Daughters of the Cord run a hospice near the salt pans. They ask no payment—but they cut threads. A renegade Sister hides among the herders, trying to untie her cord from a Ykrul blood-weight.
  • The Threshold Compendium: The kurgans are Class II Anchor sites—the spirits of old Khagans bound by unfinished wergild. Saikou Ira would carry a bag of salt in each hand here.
  • Malachai, the Chained Angel: A silver coin that never spends circulates among the salt-brokers. Take it, and your store never runs dry—but the coin will reappear in the salt pan at every new moon.
  • The Gray Wanderer's Grimoire: Ykrul psions are called “Weather-Readers.” They taste the future in the wind. Road-judges employ two—and have never trusted a verdict they didn't hear from the sky.

Ykrul of the Eastern Steppe — The Iron Grass Clans

The wind is colder here, and so are the hearts. We do not forget an insult because we cannot afford to. The grass is thinner, the wolves are bolder, and the sky speaks only in threats. The western Ykrul trade in stories. We trade in teeth.

Eastern Khatun (Elite):

“The westerners count their weights in silk and verses. We count ours in frost and iron. A broken oath here is not a story for the skald—it is a hole in the winter larder. We do not forgive because we cannot. The Hollow rides with us every night. We have no room for mercy, and even less for poetry. Kon'reh is still our teacher, but the board is frozen, and the pieces are bones.”

The Eastern Ykrul share blood with their Violet Steppe cousins, but generations on the harsher eastern ranges—where the Kuvani horse clans also roam—have carved them into a different shape. They are more isolationist, more pragmatic, and their honor code has less room for talk. They live among the Kuvani but do not intermarry; they trade when the snows allow, raid when the grass is thin, and speak a dialect that grinds like stones in a riverbed. Outsiders call them the Iron Grass Clans. They call themselves Those Who Remember Winter.

Eastern Variations (Use with Ykrul Core)

When generating an Eastern Ykrul camp, clan, or encounter, roll on the tables below instead of (or in addition to) the standard Ykrul generators.

Eastern Clan Trait (d6)

  1. Frost-Bitten: The clan survives on the edge of the permafrost. +1 die to Survival in cold weather, but –1 die to all social rolls with lowlanders (they stink of frozen horse).
  2. Kuvani-Kin: This clan has traded with the Kuvani for generations. They speak the Kuvani tongue and may call on one Kuvani remount station per journey. Rivals whisper they have forgotten the old ways.
  3. Wolf-Pacted: The clan has a standing truce with a local wolf pack. Wolves do not attack their herds. In exchange, the clan leaves every ninth calf unbranded—for the wolves. The pack sometimes fights beside them in battle (Cap 2, appears once per arc).
  4. Salt-Misers: This clan controls a small, bitter salt pan. They trade salt for iron with the Kuvani. They are wealthy but paranoid; any visitor is assumed to be a spy. +1 die to detect deception, but starting Position in parley is Desperate.
  5. Curse-Bearers: A broken oath from three generations ago still hangs over the clan. Their horses are prone to sickness, and their children are born with gray eyes. They seek a way to lift the curse—and will do almost anything to find it.
  6. Road-Breakers: This clan claims the right to open or close the high passes east. They carry a brass key (actually a bronze paiza) that Kuvani caravans recognize. They are isolationist, proud, and quick to draw steel over matters of passage.

Eastern Steppe Hazards (d6)

When traveling through Eastern Ykrul territory, roll for a hazard (or replace a standard Clubs draw).

  1. Frost-Rime Grass: The grass is coated in a crystalline frost that cuts horse legs. Travel speed halved for one leg. On a failed Survival roll, one mount goes lame (mark 1 Fatigue or lose 1 Supply).
  2. Wolf Howl at Dusk: A pack shadows the party for a day and a night. They will not attack unless the party is weak or separated. If a character sleeps alone, roll Spirit+Resolve (DV 3) or be found by wolves at midnight.
  3. Ice Lens Mirage: The flat steppe plays tricks. Navigation rolls suffer –1 die for the leg. On a miss, the party wanders into a kurgan field—the dead are restless. Start a Spirit Attention [4] timer.
  4. Salt Pan Crack: A dried salt pan has a hidden fissure. A horse or wagon breaks through. Lose 1 Supply (salt pork ruined) and mark 1 Fatigue for the party as they pull the animal free.
  5. Kuvani Border Markers: The party has crossed into a Kuvani grazing ground without permission. Kuvani riders appear (Cap 2, non-hostile unless provoked). They demand a story or a small offering of salt. Refusal starts a Border Dispute [4] timer.
  6. Silent Sky: No birds, no wind, no insect sounds. The Hollow is near. For the next scene, any roll that shows a 1 also summons a Oath-Keeper—it will appear at the end of the scene to ask one question. Answer poorly, and it takes a memory.

Eastern Ykrul Encounter (d8)

When meeting an Eastern Ykrul camp or patrol, roll for their initial disposition and a twist.

  1. Hungry Hosts: The camp is low on supplies. They will trade—at ruinous prices. But they will also remember a fair deal. If you overpay (give 1 extra Supply), gain a String (Eastern Ykrul favor).
  2. Grieving Clan: A child died last night. The camp is in mourning. They will not trade or talk. If you offer a true story about a lost loved one, they will share a meal.
  3. Feud-Watch: The clan is on alert for raiders from a rival banner. They mistake the party for scouts. The first social roll is at Desperate Position. A successful Deception or Bribe (cost 1 Boon or 1 Supply) clears the misunderstanding.
  4. Salt-Broker's Caravan: A small caravan of salt traders, heavily armed. They are willing to trade salt for news of the west. They ask pointed questions about the Violet Steppe—they are planning a raid.
  5. Kurgan Pilgrims: A group of bone-singers and their guards are traveling to a sacred kurgan. They will allow the party to accompany them if the party helps perform a ritual: each member must whisper a forgotten name into a horse skull. Afterwards, the party gains +1 die to resist fear for one day, but the Hollow knows one of your names.
  6. Storm-Singer's Challenge: A sky-speaker challenges the party to a weather-calling contest. Roll Spirit+Lore vs. DV 4. On a success, the storm-singer is impressed and grants a Sky's Exception (ignore one weather hazard). On a failure, the party is drenched and loses 1 Fatigue.
  7. Hollow-Touched Camp: The camp is silent. Everyone is sitting still, staring at a single point. A Oath-Keeper has passed through. If the party can speak the name of the broken oath, the camp will wake. Otherwise, they must leave an offering of salt and retreat.
  8. Iron Grass Market: A rare gathering of three clans at a neutral ford. The market lasts only one day. Goods for sale: remounts (cost 1 Supply each), salt (1 Supply per 2 portions), iron arrowheads (1 Boon for 1 die bonus on next ranged attack), and one cursed paiza (half price, but the previous owner's ghost follows).

Eastern Ykrul Customs (d6)

When interacting with an Eastern Ykrul camp, roll for a local custom that may affect social rolls.

  1. No Left Hand: Offering anything with the left hand is an insult. All social rolls using Presence+Sway suffer –1 die unless you explicitly use your right hand.
  2. Fire First, Talk Later: Guests must warm their hands at the central fire before speaking. If you refuse, the host will be offended—the first request you make is automatically refused.
  3. The Empty Saddle: One saddle in the camp is always empty. It belongs to a rider who died last winter. If you sit in it, you must tell a story about a death you witnessed. If the story is true, the ghost may give you a warning (GM reveals one future hazard). If false, the ghost haunts you for a day (you gain the Haunted condition).
  4. No Naming the Dead: Eastern Ykrul do not speak the names of the deceased for one year after death. If you accidentally name a dead person, the clan demands a blood-price: 1 Supply or a lock of your hair.
  5. Salt Before Meat: A meal is not blessed until the host throws a pinch of salt into the fire. If you eat before the salt, you are considered an oath-breaker. The meal will not nourish you (you do not recover Fatigue from that rest).
  6. The Ninth Breath: When drinking fermented mare's milk, you must pause after the eighth sip. If you take a ninth, you must immediately name an enemy. The clan will consider that enemy as their own for one season. The enemy may not appreciate this.

Eastern Ykrul Superstitions (burn for +1 die once)

Add these to the standard Ykrul superstitions when in the eastern steppe.

  • Iron to the Frost: Press a cold iron nail into the ground before a white squall. Ignore the first Frost-Rime Grass penalty.
  • Braid the Tail: Tie a single knot in a horse's tail hair. Cancel Wolf Howl at Dusk or take –1 die on your next survival roll (your choice).
  • Name to the Hollow: Whisper a dead enemy's name into a salt pan at midnight. Gain +1 Effect when evading Oath-Keepers this scene, but mark Obligation to the Eastern Khatun.

Eastern Ykrul Boss Seed (d6)

When the party faces a major antagonist from the Eastern Ykrul, roll for a unique foe.

  1. The Frost-Khagan (Undying): A chieftain who froze to death but did not stop riding. Phases: Blizzard's Call $$ Frozen Host $$ The Long Night. Cracks: burn a horse tooth in a fire, speak his true name (carved on his saddle), or offer a living rider to take his place.
  2. The Salt-Throat (Cursed Broker): A salt merchant who sold his clan's future for wealth. Phases: Weigh the Debt $$ Salt Tithe $$ The Scales Close. Cracks: pay a wergild in untainted salt, expose his false paiza, or cut his hostage string with a silver knife.
  3. The Hollow's Rider (Oath-Keeper in Flesh): A warrior who made a pact with the Hollow to survive a winter famine. Phases: Count the Breath $$ Ride the White $$ Claim the Ninth. Cracks: feed him a memory of warmth, show him a red thread tied in eight knots, or outride him to the horizon—the Hollow cannot cross moving water.
  4. The Iron Grandmother (Bone-Singer Revenant): An ancient bone-singer who refused to die. Phases: Song of Names $$ Kurgan Rising $$ The Dead Vote. Cracks: sing a true name she does not know, return a stolen kurgan stone, or answer her riddle: “What weight is never paid?”
  5. The Wolf-Banner (Pack-Leader): A clan chief who has fused with a wolf spirit. Phases: Stalk $$ Circle $$ Feast. Cracks: break his banner, offer a raw heart to his pack, or survive a night without fire.
  6. The Sky-Weeping Child (Curse Bearer): A child born during a white squall, marked by the Hollow. Phases: Winter Dreams $$ Frost-Fever $$ The Ninth Day. Cracks: warm her hands with your own breath, speak her true name (her mother is still alive—she will tell you), or find the horse that was born at the same moment—it carries the other half of the curse.

Eastern Steppe Names (d8 each)

For quick NPC generation, roll or choose:

Male first names (d8):

1. Bortei, 2. Temurjin, 3. Khorchi, 4. Edil, 5. Sogata, 6. Toghrul, 7. Ogedei, 8. Chagatai.

Female first names (d8):

1. Orghana, 2. Borte, 3. Altan, 4. Saruul, 5. Checheg, 6. Nergui, 7. Tolui, 8. Hulan.

Clan epithets (d8):

1. Iron-Heart, 2. Frost-Mane, 3. Salt-Tongue, 4. Wolf-Brood, 5. Hollow-Sight, 6. Curse-Bearer, 7. Red-Hoof, 8. Silent-Banner.

Eastern Ykrul at a Glance

Core Traits: Isolationist, pragmatic, harsher honor code, live among Kuvani but do not intermarry.

Key Resources: Salt, iron, remounts, wolf truces, kurgan spirits.

Common Conflicts: Feuds over salt pans, disputes with Kuvani over grazing, Hollow incursions in winter, curse-lifting quests.

Tone: Bleaker than western Ykrul. Fewer stories, more teeth. Hospitality is still sacred—but the guest is expected to earn it.

Ritual Objects: Frost-bitten stirrups, bronze paizas, horse-tooth chits, salt-cakes, wolf skull standards.

Patronage & Power

In Ykrul society, power flows through the careful management of hospitality, the binding of oaths with hostages, and the ability to navigate the complex web of tribal relationships. The Khagan maintains authority through the loyalty of noyans and the respect of khatuns, while local leaders wield influence through their control of resources like salt, remounts, and safe passage. The true power lies with those who understand the old calculations—the pragmatic reckonings of Kon'reh—and can maintain the delicate balance between hospitality and honor.

For the GM:

Patronage in Ykrul society revolves around hospitality, hostage exchanges, and the ability to command respect from hosts and courts. Rewards often take the form of tokens, treaties, and official positions that can be leveraged into greater influence. To emphasize the old ways:

  • Tie rewards to visible symbols (tokens, braids, standards) that can be challenged, stolen, or voided.
  • Use the Bowl and Board procedure for disputes—first fairness (Bowl), then routes and concessions (Board), then a seasonal right.
  • Let rival hosts issue conflicting protections, forcing players to choose whose favor matters more.
  • Remember that Ykrul pragmatism is colder than southern sentiment. An ally is an asset; a debtor is a liability. Mercy is a luxury, not a virtue. The game of Kon'reh teaches that a trapped piece is dead weight—free it or sacrifice it, but never weep for it.

In Ykrul society, your word is your bond, and your bond determines whether you ride as guest or ghost.

}
spades: Wolf Milepost [Travel]. Stacked stones; wind whines through eye-holes. Each stone is a tally. Remove one, and the wind names you debtor.
Remount Station [Travel]. Low corral with stamped snow and tether-posts. The tether-posts are carved with the names of dead horses. The living ones remember.
Birch Windbreak [Ritual]. Above black-earth tracks; old offerings in the bark. The offerings are strips of wool. Each strip is a prayer for safe crossing.
Salt Pan [Weight]. Crusted white; hoof-prints like stars across the void. The pan has a name: “The Khagan's Mirror.” Look into it, and you see what you owe.
Reed Ford [Travel]. River braids; poles mark yesterday's safe line. The poles shift in the night. The river's path changes with the moon.
Trading Palisade [Trade]. Way-post with wolf-skull pennons snapping. The skulls face outward. They watch for liars and bad weights.
Winter Camp [Social]. Felt tents in a horseshoe facing the wind. The open end is for guests. The closed end is for the ancestors.
Kurgan Field [Death]. Low barrows; horse-bones bead the grass like prayer beads. The bones were placed by a bone-singer. Each bead is a name.
Watch Kopje [Weather]. Lonely tor with signal fire-scar; the eye of the steppe. The fire-scar is from a signal that saved a host. The smoke still smells of hope.
Pontoon Crossing [Travel]. Laced hides creak; the current speaks fast and hungry. The hides are from horses that died crossing. Their ghosts still pull.
Council Hollow [Social]. Standards planted; the ground tamped like a war-drum. The standards have ears. Speak a treason here, and every clan will know by dawn.
Khagan's Station [Authority]. Broad ger on a timber platform; guards in silence. The guards never blink. They are paid in salt and secrets.
Sky Steppe [Mystery]. Open, wind like a voice; tracks keep pace beside you. The tracks are not yours. They belong to the ancestors who ride with you.
hearts: Herd-Scout [Travel]. Three ponies and six opinions; the horizon as a map. He can read a trail by the way the grass bends. He can read a liar by the way a rider checks his exits.
Camp-Mother [Social]. Chooses where fires live; hospitality is law. She has never drawn a blade. She has never needed to.
Banner Youth [Combat]. Minding the wolves' tooth standard; pride as duty. The standard is older than his grandmother's grandmother. He will die before it falls.
Salt-Broker [Trade]. Weighs promises like grain; trade as precision. Her scales are honest—because a dishonest scale is a broken tool, and tools are expensive.
Remount Keeper [Travel]. String-master with a book of brands; horses as currency. His book is written in hoof-prints. He can read a horse's soul.
Bone-Singer [Death]. Knows the kurgans' proper names; death as memory. She sings the names of the dead each dawn. The dead sing back.
Road-Judge [Law]. Tümen scribe with seals and a patient ear. His seals are from three empires. He uses whichever one is still valid.
Noyan Envoy [Social]. Silver paiza, iron smile; diplomacy as a blade. His paiza was a gift from a Khagan who is now dead. He does not mention that.
Host Captain [Combat]. The tent-wall is a map; strategy in every fold. He can see a battle in the way smoke rises. He is usually right.
Falcon Courier [Travel]. Leather gauntlets, answers sooner than asked. The falcon is a sleeper agent. It belongs to a different clan. The courier doesn't know.
Khatun of Ring [Law]. Keeper of camp-law and hostage strings. Her strings are braided from the hair of hostages. She knows each strand's owner.
Khagan's Kin [Social]. A nephew or niece sent to bind a foedus with a look. They have never lost a negotiation. They have never needed to speak.
Sky-Speaker [Magic]. Shaman of storms; shortcuts owe them a price. They can call lightning—or call it off. The price is always a story.
clubs: White Squall [Weather]. Dry snow, lost horizons; navigation by gut and horse sense. The snow tastes of iron. Someone is bleeding nearby.
Rasputitsa [Weather]. The road becomes porridge; wheels are lies on the earth. The mud has a smell—old blood and older lies.
Remount Sickness [Death]. A cough in the string; swaps get awkward, trust thinner. The cough is a curse. The horses know who cast it.
Salt Shortage [Weight]. Meat spoils; tempers thin like winter air. The salt was cut with grave dust. Someone wanted the winter to be hard.
Hostage Call [Social]. Protocol invoked; someone must sit in the tent. The tent has no floor. The hostage can see the earth beneath—and the bones.
Feud Spark [Weight]. A name spoken wrong reopens an old debt; blood is the only coin that pays. The name was spelled correctly. The insult was in the pause before it.
Grassfire [Disaster]. Runs with the wind faster than horses; escape as a prayer. The fire has a face. It is the face of a Khagan who burned a village.
Foedus Recall [Law]. Treaty terms re-read; your papers disagree. The treaty was written on both sides. The other side used a different ink.
River Break [Travel]. Ice goes; ferries don't; crossing becomes a gamble. The ice is still thick in the middle. The edges are lying.
Raid Shadow [Combat]. Another banner follows at a patient distance. They are not attacking. They are waiting for you to make a mistake.
Kurultai Call [Social]. Camps converge; travel becomes politics. The kurultai has a list of grievances. Your name is near the top.
Wolf Muster [Authority]. A levy raised; every road a checkpoint, every rider a judge. The muster horn is a wolf's skull. It sounds like a scream.
Sky Omen [Omen]. Three suns, or none; routes rewrite themselves. The omen is a wedge of cranes flying north in winter. The sky is wrong.
diamonds: Camp Token [Social]. One night's lawful fire and fodder; hospitality as a right. The token is a horsehair braid. Burn it to claim your fire.
Salt Allotment [Trade]. Draw from a named store without quarrel. The allotment is a salt-cake. Break it in half to share.
Ford-Right [Travel]. Cross a named braid at lawful depth. The right is a pole. Plant it in the river, and the ford will hold—once.
Remount Chit [Travel]. Swap two tired mounts for fresh. The chit is a horse's tooth. The remount keeper will not smile when you present it.
Escort Braid [Travel]. Two riders see you through a bad stretch. The braid is woven from the riders' own hair. They will die before they cut it.
Safe-Hostage [Social]. Place or take token-kin for a truce. The hostage is a child. The child is patient.
Paiza Tablet [Authority]. Priority on the Wolf Road and at posts. The tablet is bronze. It is warm when your reputation is good.
Foedus Seal [Law]. A treaty clause that trumps petty order. The seal is a wolf's head. Press it into wax, and the steppe listens.
Market-Green [Trade]. Trade under a banner; weapons sheathed. The banner is a green field. No blood may be spilled beneath it.
Standard Guard [Combat]. Travel under wolf-tooth; raiders defer. The standard is a wolf's tooth on a pole. Raiders will bow—or die.
Ring Audience [Social]. The Khatun hears you alone; precedent sticks. The audience is held at midnight. The fire is the only witness.
Khagan's Writ [Authority]. Doors open, mouths close across the steppe. The writ is a sheet of hammered silver. It bends, but it does not break.
Sky's Exception [Weather]. Once, the weather ignores you (storm, whiteout, dust). The exception is a single breath. Use it well.
